guidance: remove formulaic connectors (however, furthermore, moreover, in conclusion); vary sentence length sharply; mix very short and long sentences
original_text:
The controversy surrounding the landform on Mars that appears to resemble a face has captured the imagination of space enthusiasts and conspiracy theorists alike. However, I firmly believe that the "Face on Mars" is simply a natural landform and not evidence of extraterrestrial life.

One of the main arguments in favor of the natural origin of the landform is the concept of pareidolia. Pareidolia is the psychological phenomenon where our brains perceive familiar patterns, such as faces, in random stimuli. This is the same reason why we see shapes in clouds or faces in random patterns. The human brain is wired to recognize and interpret faces, even in ambiguous or abstract forms. Therefore, it is not surprising that when presented with a rocky landform on Mars with two depressions for eyes and a prominent ridge for a nose, we see a face.

Moreover, additional evidence supporting the natural origin of the landform comes from NASA's Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter, which captured high-resolution images of the area. These images reveal that the "Face on Mars" is actually an eroded mesa called the Cydonia region. The mesa is a result of natural geological processes, such as wind erosion, which can create symmetrical features resembling a face.

Furthermore, the area surrounding the mesa also shows signs of other geological formations, such as ridges, valleys, and craters. These formations are consistent with the known geological processes on Mars, such as impacts from asteroids and the Martian wind. This suggests that the "Face on Mars" is just one of many naturally occurring landforms on the planet, rather than a solitary evidence of extraterrestrial life.

While the idea of discovering extraterrestrial life is intriguing, it is essential to approach such claims with a critical mindset and rely on evidence-based explanations. In the case of the "Face on Mars," the evidence overwhelmingly points towards a natural landform rather than an indication of intelligent beings. Science thrives on skepticism and rigorous inquiry, and it is through this approach that we can continue to explore and uncover the mysteries of our universe.
rewritten_text:
That face on Mars has gripped space enthusiasts and conspiracy theorists alike for decades. It's a rock.

Pareidolia is the go-to explanation for anyone arguing the landform has a natural origin. Pareidolia is what happens when your brain insists it sees a face — or some other familiar shape — in something completely random. This is the same reason why we see shapes in clouds or faces in random patterns. The human brain is wired to recognize and interpret faces, even in ambiguous or abstract forms. A rocky Martian landform with two shadowed depressions and a jutting central ridge — of course we see a face.

The Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter ran its high-resolution cameras across the area, and everything it captured pointed back to the same conclusion: nature did this. What those images actually show is an eroded mesa — the Cydonia region, nothing more. That mesa got worked over by wind and centuries until it became something unsettling — hit the right angle and it's a face, staring straight back.

Ridges, valleys, and craters cut across the terrain in every direction around the mesa. The formations line up with what we already know drives geology on Mars — asteroid impacts and the relentless Martian wind. The "Face on Mars" is, it turns out, nothing more than one landform among countless others scattered across the planet — not some lone relic of alien life.

Finding extraterrestrial life would be one of the most profound discoveries in human history — but wanting it to be true has never made anything true, and every serious claim, no matter how thrilling, still has to survive the same hard, evidence-driven scrutiny we'd apply to anything else. The "Face on Mars" is just a rock. Science stays alive because we keep asking hard questions and refuse to stop reaching.
